title: Zornia heterophylla
taxonomy: Zornia heterophylla is classified within the family Fabaceae, commonly known as the legume or pea family. This classification places Zornia heterophylla among the approximately 19,000 known species of flowering plants in this family. Further, Zornia heterophylla belongs to the genus Zornia, which comprises approximately 130 species of flowering plants. The genus Zornia is part of the subfamily Faboideae, which is one of the largest subfamilies within the Fabaceae family. This classification reflects the evolutionary relationships and shared characteristics among these plant species.
uses: Zornia heterophylla has been traditionally used in folk medicine in various parts of the world. Its leaves and stems are reported to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. In some cultures, the plant is used to treat fever, cough, and respiratory issues. However, more research is needed to confirm its efficacy and potential side effects. Traditional uses should not be used as a substitute for modern medical treatment without consulting a healthcare professional.

intro: Zornia heterophylla, also known as "Macha" or "Tremo", is a perennial legume native to the tropical regions of Central and South America, characterized by its sprawling habit and small, white or pink flowers. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant effects, which may aid in the treatment of various conditions such as fever, digestive issues, and respiratory infections. The bioactive constituents of Zornia heterophylla include flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins, which are thought to be responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the plant is often prepared as an infusion, decoction, or tincture, and is used in herbal remedies for its purported health benefits, although its effectiveness and safety have not been extensively scientifically studied.
